title: CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management

sections.heading: Scope and Purpose
sections.content: CPS 230 establishes APRA's requirements for the management of operational risk by all APRA-regulated entities. The standard applies to all material operational processes, including those involving automated systems, artificial intelligence, and machine learning models. Operational resilience requires that entities maintain the capacity to deliver critical operations during periods of stress and that all material risk decisions are subject to appropriate human oversight. CPS 230 came into full effect on 1 July 2025 and supersedes earlier operational risk guidance that did not explicitly address AI and automated decision systems.

sections.heading: Artificial Intelligence and Automated Decision Systems
sections.content: APRA has confirmed that the use of artificial intelligence in material operational processes — including credit risk assessment, portfolio monitoring, and regulatory compliance — is subject to CPS 230. An AI system used to assess credit risk, identify regulatory breaches, or produce risk recommendations must meet the following standards: (a) its outputs must be explainable and auditable; (b) every material recommendation must be accompanied by an evidence trail linking the recommendation to underlying data; (c) the system must not make autonomous decisions that would normally require human authorisation; and (d) human oversight must be embedded as an architectural requirement, not merely an optional review step.

sections.heading: Human Oversight — The Co-Pilot Requirement
sections.content: APRA's position on AI in banking is explicit: AI can be a valuable co-pilot, but it must never be an autopilot. An ADI using AI for credit risk intelligence must implement a mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoint before any AI-generated risk recommendation is finalised or communicated to decision-makers. This requirement is not satisfied by a post-hoc review of AI outputs — the human oversight must occur before the recommendation is presented as a concluded finding. An AI risk intelligence system that generates a risk brief without a human approval step is not compliant with CPS 230, regardless of the accuracy of its findings. The human-in-the-loop checkpoint must be implemented at the architectural level, not merely as a procedural control.

sections.heading: Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions
sections.content: Every material AI-generated recommendation must be supported by a complete audit trail that enables post-hoc review of the reasoning process. The audit trail must include: (a) the specific data records consulted by the AI system; (b) the reasoning steps taken by the AI to reach its recommendation; (c) the confidence level assigned to each finding; (d) any data gaps or uncertainties acknowledged by the system; (e) the identity of the human who reviewed and approved the recommendation; and (f) the timestamp of both the AI recommendation and the human approval. An AI system that generates findings without citing specific TRBK table records as evidence does not meet the audit trail requirement of CPS 230. APRA examiners will assess AI audit trail completeness as part of operational resilience reviews from 2026.

sections.heading: Board and Senior Management Accountability
sections.content: The Board of an ADI is responsible for ensuring that AI systems used in material operational processes are transparent, auditable, and subject to appropriate human governance. Senior management must approve the use of any AI system in credit risk assessment and must receive regular reporting on AI system performance, including accuracy, confidence levels, and any instances where AI confidence fell below acceptable thresholds. The Board must be notified of any AI system output that results in a regulatory breach finding or a recommendation to report to APRA. AI system governance must be included in the ADI's operational risk management framework and reviewed annually. A Board that has not explicitly considered and approved AI use in credit risk assessment is not compliant with CPS 230.

sections.heading: Operational Resilience and Business Continuity
sections.content: AI systems used in critical operational processes must meet the same business continuity standards as other critical technology infrastructure. This includes: (a) state persistence across system restarts — an AI system that loses its assessment state when the application restarts is not operationally resilient; (b) documented recovery procedures; (c) defined maximum tolerable disruption periods for AI-assisted risk processes; and (d) tested failover to manual processes where the AI system is unavailable. PostgreSQL state persistence for AI agent workflows is an example of an architectural choice that supports CPS 230 operational resilience requirements. MemorySaver implementations that reset on application restart do not meet CPS 230 resilience standards for production use.
